name: Albania
content:
The Albanian Football Federation (FSHF) was established on 6 June 1930 when a royal decree by King Zog I formally recognized the Albanian Sports Federation. Initially operating as a branch of that body, FSHF joined FIFA at its June Congress in 1932, marking Albania's entry into international football. A further reorganisation in March 1934 transferred authority to "Vllaznija Shqiptare", a national sports body overseeing multiple athletic disciplines.

A landmark moment came in 1954 when Albania became one of the founding members of UEFA, cementing its place in European football governance. The federation was restructured into its current independent form in February 1962. Since 2002, Armand Duka has served as president, winning re-election across multiple terms.

The federation has not been without difficulties. In March 2008 FIFA suspended FSHF due to heavy political interference, barring national teams from official competition. The suspension was lifted in May 2008 once the political issues were resolved. Disputes over the Qemal Stafa Stadium emerged in 2009, and a 2011 agreement established joint ownership arrangements for a proposed replacement facility estimated at 60 million euros.

Headquartered in Tirana, the FSHF today oversees multiple domestic league tiers and manages national teams across senior, youth, and women's categories.

name: Andorra
content:
The Andorran Football Federation (FAF) was founded in 1994 and admitted to both FIFA and UEFA in 1996, making it one of the younger European football associations. Headquartered in Escaldes-Engordany, the federation governs all forms of football in the small Pyrenean principality of Andorra.

The FAF organises domestic competition across several tiers, including the Primera Divisió, the Segona Divisió, the Copa Constitució, and the Andorran Supercup. In addition to traditional eleven-a-side football, the federation oversees futsal and beach soccer programmes.

On the international stage the federation manages national teams across multiple categories: the senior men's and women's sides, as well as youth squads at U-21, U-19, and U-17 levels. Given Andorra's small population, the national team has historically faced significant challenges in competitive play, yet the federation continues to develop grassroots football and invest in the long-term growth of the sport in the country.

name: Armenia
content:
The Football Federation of Armenia (FFA) was established on 18 January 1992 following the country's declaration of independence from the Soviet Union in 1991. The same year, the FFA was admitted to both FIFA and UEFA, rapidly integrating Armenia into the international football community.

The national team made their competitive debut in Euro 1996 qualifying, suffering a 2–0 defeat against Belgium on 7 September 1994. Their first competitive victory came on 6 September 1995 with a 2–1 win over Macedonia. A notable recent highlight was finishing third in their UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying group, a campaign during which midfielder Henrikh Mkhitaryan established himself as a prominent figure in European football.

On the infrastructure front, the FFA opened a national training centre with residential facilities in September 2010, having begun construction in 2007. With support from FIFA and the Armenian government, approximately 90 small stadiums have been built across the country to promote grassroots development. In June 2022, FIFA President Gianni Infantino visited Armenia to mark the federation's 30th anniversary and pledged support for the construction of a new national stadium.

name: Austria
content:
The Austrian Football Association (ÖFB) was founded on 18 March 1904, making it one of the oldest football federations in the world. Austria joined FIFA in 1905 and even hosted the fifth FIFA Congress in 1908. The roots of Austrian football stretch back to 1894, when the First Vienna Football Club became the country's first organised team.

The most celebrated chapter in Austrian football history is the "Wunderteam" era of the late 1920s and early 1930s, shaped by coach and general-secretary Hugo Meisl. The side went unbeaten across 14 successive matches between April 1931 and October 1932, recording stunning victories such as 6–0 and 5–0 against Germany. This golden generation also reached the semi-finals of the 1934 World Cup, finishing fourth. Two years later, Austria claimed a silver medal at the 1936 Summer Olympics, losing the final 2–1 to Italy.

A second golden age arrived in the 1950s, driven by world-class talents Ernst Ocwirk, Ernst Happel, and Gerhard Hanappi. Austria's 1954 World Cup campaign is widely regarded as one of their finest performances on the global stage. Austria became a UEFA founding member in 1954 and has since participated in numerous World Cups, including appearances in 1978, 1982, 1990, and 1998.

Today the ÖFB is Austria's largest sporting organisation, with over 285,000 registered players across 2,309 clubs, and continues to develop football at all levels throughout the country.

name: Azerbaijan
description: Association of Football Federations of Azerbaijan. Founded: 1992.
content:
The Association of Football Federations of Azerbaijan (AFFA) was established on 26 March 1992 following Azerbaijan's independence from the Soviet Union. The federation joined both FIFA and UEFA in 1994, marking the country's formal entry into international football.

The federation's early years were not without turbulence. In April 2002, UEFA imposed a two-year ban on AFFA due to serious conflicts with domestic clubs — the national championship was abandoned and top clubs blocked players from representing the national team, delivering a significant setback to Azerbaijani football's development.

Recovery and modernisation followed in subsequent years. In 2009, AFFA unveiled the Azerbaijan Football Academy on Nobel Avenue in Baku, with UEFA vice president Şenes Erzik attending the opening ceremony. The federation adopted a new organisational identity in 2010 and has since invested in infrastructure and youth development across the country.

AFFA oversees the Azerbaijan Premier League, the Azerbaijan Cup, and futsal competitions, as well as national teams across all age groups and gender categories. Rovshan Najaf has served as president since 2024, succeeding Rovnag Abdullayev who led the federation from 2008 to 2024.

name: Belarus
content:
The Football Federation of Belarus (BFF) was established in 1989 and serves as the governing body for association football, beach soccer, and futsal in Belarus. The federation gained FIFA membership in 1992, enabling participation in World Cup qualification, and joined UEFA in 1993, opening access to European Championship and club competitions.

Headquartered in Minsk, the BFF oversees the Belarusian Premier League as well as the men's and women's national teams and futsal competitions. The country produced a generation of notable players, with the 1990s and 2000s seeing Belarusian talent compete at the highest levels of European club football.

In recent years the federation has faced significant political pressure. In June 2021 UEFA suspended Belarus from hosting any events or competitions under its jurisdiction. Following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022, both FIFA and UEFA imposed further restrictions on Belarusian teams, including bans from international competition and hosting rights, casting uncertainty over the federation's standing in European football.

name: Belgium
content:
The Royal Belgian Football Association (RBFA) was founded on 1 September 1895, making it one of the oldest football governing bodies in the world. It was a founding member of FIFA in 1904 and of UEFA in 1954, reflecting Belgium's long-standing influence in international football governance. The association was originally headquartered in Brussels before relocating to Tubize in October 2021.

The RBFA administers the full Belgian football pyramid, including the First and Second Divisions, the Belgian Cup, the Supercup, women's leagues, futsal, and eSports competitions. It also manages national teams across all age groups and genders.

On the international stage, Belgium's greatest achievement came at the 1986 FIFA World Cup, where the national team reached the semi-finals. That tournament also inspired a lasting social initiative: the RBFA supported the Casa Hogar project in Mexico for street children, contributing to the meals and education of 500 children over 25 years. In 1992, the federation received the FIFA Fair Play Award in recognition of its values off the pitch.

In the modern era, Belgium assembled one of Europe's most talented generations — nicknamed the "Golden Generation" — reaching the World Cup semi-finals again in 2018 and consistently ranking among the top sides in the FIFA world rankings.

name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
description: Football Association of Bosnia and Herzegovina. Founded: 1920.
content:
The Football Association of Bosnia and Herzegovina (N/FSBiH) traces its roots to 1920, when the Sarajevo football sub-association was established. Football itself arrived in the region far earlier — official competition began in 1908 under Austro-Hungarian rule. For much of the 20th century the association operated within the Yugoslav football system, producing celebrated players such as Safet Sušić and Vahid Halilhodžić.

Following Bosnia and Herzegovina's declaration of independence, the federation was refounded in 1992 and admitted to both FIFA and UEFA. However, its governance reflected the ethnic power-sharing structure of the Dayton Agreement, with a unique three-person presidency representing Bosniaks, Bosnian Serbs, and Bosnian Croats — an arrangement that persisted for nearly two decades.

In April 2011, FIFA and UEFA suspended the association with immediate effect due to non-compliance with statutes requiring a single president. The suspension was lifted on 30 May 2011 after a FIFA-appointed normalisation committee, headed by legendary coach Ivica Osim, restructured the organisation. In December 2012, Elvedin Begić became the federation's first single president, marking a new era of unified governance.

Today the association oversees the Premier League of Bosnia and Herzegovina, national teams across all age groups and genders, and futsal competitions. The national team reached the FIFA World Cup for the first time in 2014, a landmark achievement in the country's football history.

name: Bulgaria
content:
The Bulgarian Football Union (BFU) was founded in 1923 as the football department of the Bulgarian National Sports Federation. It affiliated with FIFA in 1924 and became a UEFA founding member in 1954. Over the decades the organisation underwent several name changes before adopting its current title on 27 June 1985.

Bulgaria's greatest footballing moment came at the 1994 FIFA World Cup in the United States, where the team reached the semi-finals under manager Dimitar Penev. Their campaign included a stunning 2–1 quarter-final victory over defending champions Germany, before falling to Italy in the semis. The squad was headlined by Hristo Stoichkov, who won the Ballon d'Or that same year, alongside Krasimir Balakov and Yordan Lechkov. The side finished fourth overall — the finest result in Bulgarian football history.

Earlier international honours include a silver medal at the 1968 Summer Olympics in Mexico City and a bronze at the 1956 Melbourne Games. Bulgaria also claimed three Balkan Cup titles in 1931, 1932, and 1973–76.

In recent years the federation faced turbulence both on and off the pitch. A 2019 home match against England was marred by racist chanting from a section of fans, prompting widespread criticism and the temporary resignation of then-president Borislav Mihaylov. Georgi Ivanov was elected president in March 2024 as the BFU seeks to restore stability and revive the national team's fortunes.

name: Croatia
content:
The Croatian Football Federation (HNS) was established on 14 June 1912 as the football section of the Croatian Sports Federation. After World War I it was absorbed into the Football Association of Yugoslavia, though a separate Croatian federation was briefly re-established in 1939. Following Croatian independence in 1991, the HNS was readmitted to FIFA on 3 July 1992 and to UEFA on 17 June 1993. The domestic league and cup competitions both launched in early 1992, with clubs such as Dinamo Zagreb and Hajduk Split carrying a rich tradition built during the Yugoslav era.

Croatia's national team made an extraordinary entrance onto the world stage, recording what FIFA described as the fastest ascension in ranking history — admitted at position 125 in 1994 and climbing to third place by 1999. The 1998 World Cup in France announced Croatia's arrival: the team finished third, with striker Davor Šuker claiming the Golden Boot with six goals.

The golden generation reached its peak at the 2018 World Cup in Russia, where Croatia became runners-up — the second-smallest country by population ever to reach a World Cup final — losing 4–2 to France. Luka Modrić won the Golden Ball as the tournament's best player. The team claimed bronze again at the 2022 World Cup in Qatar, defeating Morocco 2–1, and finished second in the 2023 UEFA Nations League Finals, cementing Croatia's status as one of Europe's most consistent footballing nations.

name: Cyprus
content:
The Cyprus Football Association (CFA) was established on 23 September 1934 by eight founding clubs, including APOEL, Anorthosis Famagusta, and AEL Limassol. Organised football had been introduced to Cyprus earlier in the 20th century by the British, spreading initially through school competitions before developing into a structured league system. The CFA joined FIFA in 1948 and UEFA in 1962.

The association administers the Cypriot First Division, the Cypriot Cup, the Super Cup, as well as futsal competitions including the Cypriot Futsal First Division and Futsal Cup. It also oversees the national team and youth squads across all age categories. In 2007 the CFA moved to a new headquarters in Nicosia, with the opening ceremony attended by UEFA president Michel Platini and the President of Cyprus.

Cypriot club football is dominated by a handful of historically strong clubs, with APOEL and Anorthosis Famagusta achieving notable runs in UEFA club competitions. APOEL reached the quarter-finals of the UEFA Champions League in 2012, one of the most remarkable achievements by a club from a smaller European league.

name: Czech Republic
description: Football Association of the Czech Republic. Founded: 1901.
content:
The Football Association of the Czech Republic (FAČR) was established on 19 October 1901 as the Bohemian Football Union in the Kingdom of Bohemia, then part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire — making it one of the oldest football associations in continental Europe. The organisation affiliated with FIFA in 1907 and became a UEFA founding member in 1954. It underwent several name changes reflecting the political shifts of the 20th century, including the Czechoslovak Football Association from 1922 to 1993, before adopting its current title in 2011 following the dissolution of Czechoslovakia.

The Czech national team inherited a proud tradition from its Czechoslovak predecessor, which won the European Championship in 1976 and reached two World Cup finals (1934, 1962). As an independent nation, the Czech Republic reached the UEFA Euro 1996 final — losing on a golden goal to Germany — and the semi-finals of Euro 2004, powered by a golden generation including Pavel Nedvěd, Petr Čech, Jan Koller, and Tomáš Rosický.

Off the pitch, the federation faced serious legal challenges: police raids on its headquarters in 2017 led to the arrest of chairman Miroslav Pelta, and a further raid in 2020 detained 20 individuals including vice-chairman Roman Berbr. David Trunda assumed the chairmanship in 2025 as the association sought to restore its credibility.

name: Denmark
content:
The Danish Football Union (DBU) was founded on 18 May 1889, making it the first national football association established outside Great Britain and Ireland. The DBU joined FIFA on 21 May 1904 as one of its founding members, and became a UEFA member in 1954. Based in Brøndby, the association governs professional leagues, the men's and women's national teams, and futsal, operating through six regional football associations across the country.

Denmark's greatest achievement came at UEFA Euro 1992, when the team — originally not even qualified — were invited to replace Yugoslavia following the latter's suspension due to the Yugoslav Wars. Against all odds, Denmark went on to win the tournament, defeating Germany 2–0 in the final. The squad, built around figures such as Peter Schmeichel and Brian Laudrup, delivered one of the most remarkable upsets in international football history.

In a landmark off-field development, the DBU and the players' union agreed in June 2024 to introduce equal pay terms for the men's and women's national teams — with the men's players voluntarily refusing a salary increase to fund parity, a widely praised step for gender equality in sport.

name: England
content:
The Football Association (The FA), founded on 26 October 1863 at the Freemasons' Tavern in London, is the oldest football association in the world. Its formation codified the rules of association football for the first time, separating the game from rugby — clubs that preferred handling the ball eventually broke away to form the Rugby Football Union in 1871. The FA is a founding member of FIFA (1904) and a permanent member of the International Football Association Board (IFAB), giving England a lasting voice in the Laws of the Game.

The FA Cup, created in 1872, is the longest-running association football competition in the world. England's national team won the FIFA World Cup in 1966, hosted on home soil, defeating West Germany 4–2 in the final at Wembley. In 1885 the FA legalised professional football, and in 1888 the Football League — the world's first professional league — was established in its wake. One controversial chapter was the ban on women's football imposed in 1921, which lasted until 1969.

In recent years, the England women's team claimed the UEFA European Championship in 2022 and 2025, signalling a new era of success for English football. The FA is headquartered at Wembley Stadium in London, which also serves as the national team's home ground.

name: Estonia
content:
The Estonian Football Association (EJL) was established on 14 December 1921, shortly after Estonian independence, and joined FIFA in 1923. It was one of the first independent sports governing bodies in Estonia, created when the Estonian Sports Association Kalev could no longer manage all disciplines following the country's success at the 1920 Olympics.

The federation was dissolved in 1940 following the Soviet occupation of Estonia. During the Soviet era, football was administered by a regional federation under Soviet control. The EJL was reorganised in 1988 following Estonia's sovereignty declaration and rejoined FIFA in 1991 and UEFA in 1992, subsequently re-establishing the Meistriliiga as the national top-flight competition.

Based in Tallinn, the association governs football, futsal, and beach soccer, and oversees national teams across all age groups and genders. The Estonian national team has historically struggled to qualify for major tournaments, though the federation continues to invest in youth development and infrastructure to raise the standard of the game across the country.

name: Faroe Islands
content:
The Faroe Islands Football Association (FSF) was established on 13 January 1979 in Tórshavn, though organised football in the islands dates back much further — the first Faroese national league was held in 1942, preceded by the sports association ÍSF which governed football from 1942 to 1978. The FSF gained FIFA membership on 2 July 1988 and joined UEFA on 18 April 1990.

The federation gained international attention in 1990 when the Faroe Islands national team, playing their first-ever competitive match, stunned Austria 1–0 in a UEFA Euro 1992 qualifier — one of the greatest upsets in European football history. The goal, scored by Torkil Nielsen, became part of football folklore and announced the islands' arrival on the continental stage.

Women's football developed early in the islands, with the first national women's league held in 1985. The federation gradually reduced its reliance on Danish coaching expertise through the 1980s and 1990s, building fully Faroese football structures by the mid-1990s. Today the FSF manages national teams for both men and women across various age levels and continues to develop the game in one of Europe's smallest football nations.

name: Finland
content:
The Football Association of Finland (SPL/FBF) was established on 19 May 1907 in Helsinki, joining FIFA the following year in 1908 and becoming a UEFA member in 1954. It has grown into Finland's largest amateur sports federation, with over 1,000 member clubs and approximately 140,000 registered players. The association is headquartered at Bolt Arena in Helsinki.

The SPL oversees the full national football structure, managing the second through ninth tiers of domestic competition, while the top-flight Veikkausliiga operates as a separate entity. It also administers futsal competitions and national teams across all age groups and genders. The federation has a broader historical role in Finnish sport — before specialist associations were founded, it governed bandy until 1972 and organised the first Finnish ice hockey championship in 1928.

A historic milestone came when the Finnish men's national team qualified for UEFA Euro 2020 — their first-ever major tournament appearance. The achievement marked a turning point for Finnish football, demonstrating the results of long-term investment in player development and coaching infrastructure.

name: France
content:
The French Football Federation (FFF) was established on 7 April 1919 in Paris, emerging from the transformation of the Comité Français Interfédéral. Its founding president was Jules Rimet, who would later become FIFA president and the driving force behind the creation of the FIFA World Cup. France had been a FIFA member since 1907 and became a UEFA founding member in 1954.

The French men's national team has achieved extraordinary success: two FIFA World Cup victories (1998 at home, 2018 in Russia), two UEFA European Championship titles (1984, 2000), an Olympic gold medal (1984), and runner-up finishes at the 2006 and 2022 World Cups. The 1998 tournament, held on home soil, saw France defeat Brazil 3–0 in the final with Zinedine Zidane scoring twice — a defining moment in the nation's sporting history.

France's infrastructure is among Europe's finest. The INF Clairefontaine academy, established in 1976, has produced world-class talent including Thierry Henry and Nicolas Anelka, and serves as a model for youth development globally. The federation operates 14 elite academies and supports one of the world's deepest pipelines of professional players. The women's team and youth teams have also achieved significant international results, including eight UEFA U-19 Championships and a FIFA U-20 World Cup title in 2013.

name: Georgia
content:
The Georgian Football Federation (GFF) was originally established in 1936 as part of the Soviet Union's football structure. Following Georgia's declaration of independence, the federation was re-established as an independent body on 15 February 1990 and gained both FIFA and UEFA affiliation in 1992. Headquartered in Tbilisi, the GFF oversees the Erovnuli Liga and manages national teams for men, women, and youth across all age categories, as well as futsal.

During the Soviet era, Georgian clubs — most notably Dinamo Tbilisi — were a dominant force in Soviet football, with Dinamo winning the UEFA Cup Winners' Cup in 1981, one of the most celebrated moments in Georgian football history. Players such as Kakha Kaladze and Shota Arveladze later became prominent figures in top European club football.

A landmark achievement for the independent national team came at UEFA Euro 2024, when Georgia qualified for a major tournament for the first time in their history and reached the Round of 16, defeating Portugal 2–0 in the group stage. Levan Kobiashvili has served as GFF president since 2015 and has been a key figure in the federation's modernisation and international development.

name: Germany
content:
The German Football Association (DFB) was founded on 28 January 1900 in Leipzig by representatives of 86 clubs, and joined FIFA in 1904 as the eighth member nation. With over 8 million members across 23,868 clubs as of 2025, it is the single largest sports federation in the world. The DFB is a UEFA founding member (1954) and its national team has competed at the highest level for over a century.

The men's national team has won the FIFA World Cup four times — 1954, 1974, 1990, and 2014 — and the UEFA European Championship three times: 1972, 1980, and 1996. The 1954 triumph, known as the "Miracle of Bern," saw a West Germany side widely expected to lose defeat Hungary 3–2 in the final, becoming one of football's most iconic upsets. The 2014 World Cup victory in Brazil, sealed by Mario Götze's extra-time goal against Argentina, cemented Germany's status as one of the sport's true powers.

The women's team has been equally dominant, winning the FIFA Women's World Cup in 2003 and 2007 and claiming eight UEFA Women's Championships between 1989 and 2013, as well as an Olympic gold medal in 2016. A dark chapter in the DFB's history was its conduct during the Nazi era, when Jewish players' records were erased — the federation has since made efforts to formally restore and acknowledge these histories.

name: Gibraltar
content:
The Gibraltar Football Association (GFA) was established in 1895 as the Gibraltar Civilian Football Association, making it one of Europe's oldest football bodies. A domestic league was formed in 1907 to complement the existing Merchant's Cup, and by 1901 a representative side was competing against British military teams stationed on the Rock.

The GFA's path to international recognition was lengthy and politically contested. After applying for FIFA membership in 1997, UEFA changed its statutes in 2001 to require applicants to represent UN-recognised independent states — a change widely seen as targeting Gibraltar. The Spanish Football Federation strongly opposed membership, citing historical and political claims over the territory. Two Court of Arbitration for Sport rulings, in 2003 and 2006, ultimately mandated Gibraltar's acceptance, and the GFA gained provisional UEFA status in October 2012.

On 24 May 2013, Gibraltar achieved full UEFA membership at the Congress vote — with only Belarus and Spain voting against. The occasion was marked with a commemorative 54-pence stamp recognising Gibraltar as UEFA's 54th member. FIFA membership followed on 13 May 2016, with an overwhelming 172–12 vote, immediately after Kosovo's admission, completing Gibraltar's integration into world football.

name: Greece
content:
The Hellenic Football Federation (HFF) was established on 14 November 1926 by three major football club associations to organise Greek football in line with international standards. It joined FIFA in 1927 and became a UEFA founding member in 1954. Headquartered in Athens, the HFF oversees approximately 5,773 clubs with around 2 million players, and administers national teams across men's, women's, and youth categories.

Greece's greatest footballing achievement came at UEFA Euro 2004 in Portugal, where the national team — given odds of 80/1 at the start of the tournament — defeated host Portugal in both the opening match and the final, winning 1–0 on each occasion. Coached by Otto Rehhagel, the triumph is widely considered one of the most remarkable upsets in the history of international football. The HFF also hosted the 1994 and 2007 UEFA Champions League Finals.

The federation has faced significant off-field controversy. A brief FIFA suspension in 2006 over government interference was swiftly resolved after the sports law was amended. More seriously, the "Koriopolis" match-fixing scandal and a 2015 investigation involving alleged criminal organisation within Greek football caused widespread damage to the federation's reputation and prompted major governance reforms.

name: Hungary
content:
The Hungarian Football Federation (MLSZ) was founded on 19 January 1901 in Budapest, joined FIFA in 1907, and became a UEFA founding member in 1954. Hungary is one of the pioneering nations of European football, with a rich tradition that stretches back to the early 20th century.

The pinnacle of Hungarian football was the legendary "Golden Team" (Aranycsapat) of the early 1950s, widely regarded as one of the greatest international sides ever assembled. Led by Ferenc Puskás and Sándor Kocsis, Hungary went unbeaten in 32 consecutive international matches between 1950 and 1954. They reached the 1954 World Cup final as heavy favourites but lost 3–2 to West Germany in what became known as the "Miracle of Bern." The team had also been runners-up at the 1938 World Cup, giving Hungary two World Cup final appearances overall.

In Olympic football, Hungary was a dominant force, winning gold medals in 1952, 1964, and 1968, and claiming silver in 1972 and bronze in 1960. The national team also finished third at the 1964 European Nations' Cup. After the Golden Team era, Hungarian football gradually declined, though the country's football heritage and its influence on the global game — particularly in South America — remains profound.

name: Iceland
content:
The Football Association of Iceland (KSÍ) was founded on 26 March 1947 in Reykjavík, joining FIFA the same year and becoming a UEFA member in 1954. Based in Reykjavík, the association governs domestic league football — the Úrvalsdeild — as well as national teams across men's, women's, and youth categories, plus a futsal programme.

Iceland produced one of the most celebrated underdog stories in modern football. At UEFA Euro 2016, the country became the smallest nation by population ever to qualify for and compete at a major men's tournament. They dramatically eliminated England 2–1 in the Round of 16, causing a major shock across the football world. Two years later, Iceland qualified for the 2018 FIFA World Cup — their first — further cementing the country's remarkable rise. The national team's famous "thunderclap" celebration became one of the iconic images of contemporary international football.

In 2021, the federation was rocked by a governance crisis: allegations emerged regarding mishandling of sexual assault cases involving professional players, leading to the resignation of the entire 16-member board under public and sponsor pressure. The crisis prompted significant institutional reforms in how the KSÍ addresses safeguarding and abuse reports.

name: Israel
content:
The Israel Football Association (IFA) was founded on 14 August 1928 in Mandatory Palestine as the Palestine Football Association, gaining provisional FIFA recognition in December 1928 and full membership in 1929. Following Israel's independence in 1948, the association adopted its current name. The IFA initially joined the Asian Football Confederation in 1954 but was expelled in 1974 under political pressure from Arab and Muslim member states.

For nearly two decades the IFA operated without confederation membership, unable to participate in official continental competitions. In 1992 Israel was admitted to UEFA as an associate member, becoming a full member in 1994 — a pivotal development that allowed Israeli clubs and national teams to compete in European tournaments for the first time. Headquartered in Ramat Gan, the IFA organises the Israeli Premier League, the Israel State Cup, and national teams across all age groups and genders.

The association has been the subject of ongoing international controversy related to member clubs operating in Israeli settlements in the West Bank. In 2026, FIFA sanctioned the IFA for discrimination violations, imposing a fine and requiring implementation of anti-discrimination measures, reflecting the continuing intersection of politics and sport in Israeli football.

name: Italy
content:
The Italian Football Federation (FIGC), originally known as the Federazione Italiana del Football, was established on 26 March 1898 in Turin, adopting its current name in 1909. Italy joined FIFA in 1905 and became a UEFA founding member in 1954. The national team — known as the Azzurri — wears the iconic blue jersey introduced in 1911 as a tribute to the House of Savoy.

Italy is one of the most successful nations in football history, having won the FIFA World Cup four times: 1934, 1938, 1982, and 2006. The 1982 triumph in Spain, led by Paolo Rossi who scored six goals, remains one of the most celebrated in Italian sport. The 2006 victory in Germany, sealed on penalties against France, came despite the Calciopoli match-fixing scandal engulfing Italian club football that same year. Italy also won the UEFA European Championship twice — in 1968 and memorably in 2020 (held in 2021), where they defeated England on penalties at Wembley.

Italian club football is among the richest in the world, with Juventus, AC Milan, and Inter Milan all lifting multiple European Cups. The federation oversees Serie A and the full Italian football pyramid, as well as women's football and youth competitions. The FIGC celebrated its centenary in December 1998 with a match at the Stadio Olimpico between Italy and a World XI, won 6–2 by the Azzurri.

name: Kazakhstan
content:
The Kazakhstan Football Federation (KFF) was established in 1992, reorganised from the Soviet Republican Football Association of the Kazakh SSR (founded 1989). Following independence, Kazakhstan initially joined FIFA and the Asian Football Confederation as an associate member in 1992, achieving full membership status in both organisations in 1994.

A significant shift in Kazakhstani football came in 2002, when the country transferred from the Asian Football Confederation to UEFA, becoming a full member at the UEFA Congress in Stockholm on 25 April 2002. This move aligned Kazakhstan's football with European competition structures, and since then Kazakhstani clubs and national teams have participated in UEFA qualifying and club competitions.

Headquartered in Astana, the KFF oversees the Kazakhstan Premier League and manages both men's and women's national teams. The federation has invested in youth development and international exposure, with Kazakhstan gradually strengthening its competitive record within European football. Marat Omarov has served as president since 2024.

name: Kosovo
content:
The Football Federation of Kosovo (FFK) was originally created in 1946 as a branch of the Football Association of Yugoslavia. Following Kosovo's declaration of independence on 17 February 2008, the federation restructured as a fully independent body, though initial applications to FIFA were rejected due to limited international recognition of Kosovo's statehood.

The path to full international membership was long and complex. From 2012, FIFA permitted Kosovo to play friendly matches, which helped build the federation's profile. The breakthrough came in May 2016: the FFK received UEFA membership approval on 3 May 2016, followed by FIFA membership on 13 May 2016, with 141 votes in favour and 23 against — immediately after Kosovo's admission was Gibraltar's FIFA vote, with both new members admitted on the same day.

Headquartered in Pristina, the FFK today organises football at eight competitive levels, including the Football Superleague of Kosovo, and manages national teams for men, women, youth, and futsal. Since gaining international status, the Kosovo national team has attracted several players of Kosovar heritage who previously represented other nations, building a competitive squad that has made a creditable start in UEFA qualifying competitions.

name: Latvia
content:
The Latvian Football Federation (LFF) was founded on 19 June 1921 in Riga, initially as the Latvian Football Union. A British national served as its first chairman, reflecting the English influence that spread football across the Baltic region. The federation grew rapidly — by 1922 the national championship encompassed 12 associations, 22 teams, and 479 players. Latvia joined FIFA in May 1923, and the top-flight Virslīga launched in 1927.

The federation ceased operations in 1940 following the Soviet occupation of Latvia. During the Soviet era, football was administered by a regional structure under central Soviet control. The LFF was restored on 19 August 1990 following Latvia's sovereignty declaration, and rejoined both FIFA and UEFA in 1992. The Virslīga was re-established alongside the federation's revival.

Headquartered in Riga at the Rimi Sports Centre, the LFF today manages domestic league competitions, national teams across all age groups and genders, and cup competitions. The Latvian national team has historically struggled to qualify for major tournaments, but the federation continues to invest in youth development and infrastructure to raise the competitive level of Latvian football within UEFA.

name: Liechtenstein
content:
The Liechtenstein Football Association (LFA) was established on 28 April 1934 and affiliated with both FIFA and UEFA on 22 May 1974. Headquartered in Schaan, the LFA oversees football in one of Europe's smallest nations — a principality with a population of approximately 40,000 people.

Liechtenstein holds a unique distinction among UEFA member associations: it is the only member without its own national league. Because the country has fewer than eight active clubs (excluding reserves), teams compete within the Swiss football pyramid rather than a domestic Liechtenstein competition. The LFA does, however, organise the Liechtenstein Football Cup, which provides clubs with a pathway into UEFA club competitions — FC Vaduz, the country's most prominent club, has participated regularly in the UEFA Europa League qualifiers.

Despite its size, Liechtenstein participates in all UEFA and FIFA qualifying competitions and manages national teams across multiple age groups. The national side has produced occasional upsets in qualifying campaigns, and the federation continues to develop grassroots football within the constraints of its small player pool.

name: Lithuania
content:
The Lithuanian Football Federation (LFF) was founded in 1922 and joined FIFA in 1923. Like its Baltic neighbours, the federation was dissolved following the Soviet occupation of Lithuania in 1940 and re-established in 1988 in anticipation of independence. The LFF rejoined both FIFA and UEFA in 1992, with the national top-flight A Lyga (formerly the Lithuanian A League) relaunched as the country's premier competition.

Headquartered in Vilnius, the LFF governs association football and futsal across the country. The national team has never qualified for a UEFA European Championship or FIFA World Cup, with Lithuanian football facing significant challenges in a country where basketball has historically dominated the sporting landscape. Domestic clubs have also contended with limited funding and recurring issues with betting fraud.

In spite of these challenges, the federation has achieved notable milestones in futsal: Lithuania hosted the 2021 FIFA Futsal World Cup and, together with Latvia, will co-host the UEFA Futsal Euro 2026, demonstrating the country's growing capacity for international football governance and event organisation.

name: Luxembourg
content:
The Luxembourg Football Federation (FLF) was founded on 22 November 1908 and joined FIFA just two years later in 1910, reflecting Luxembourg's early engagement with international football. The federation became a UEFA member in 1954 and is headquartered in Mondercange, south of Luxembourg City.

The FLF governs the full national football structure, including the BGL Ligue (top division), the Luxembourg Cup, and national teams across men's, women's, and youth categories. Football in Luxembourg has historically faced the challenge of competing with the country's high proportion of foreign-born residents, who have increasingly been integrated into the national team setup, strengthening the squad's competitiveness in UEFA qualifying campaigns.

In recent years, Luxembourg's national team has shown notable improvement, recording wins against higher-ranked European sides and climbing the FIFA rankings. Paul Philipp has served as federation president since 2004, overseeing a period of steady development and growing ambition within Luxembourgish football.

name: Malta
content:
The Malta Football Association (MFA) was established in 1900, making it one of Europe's oldest football governing bodies. The first national league was organised during the 1909–1910 season. Malta joined FIFA in 1959 and UEFA in 1960. The MFA is headquartered at the Ta' Qali National Stadium complex, which also houses training facilities, a gymnasium, and medical clinics.

Malta's first official international match took place in February 1957 against Austria, ending in a narrow 3–2 defeat. The national team has never qualified for a major tournament, competing in a highly competitive European environment as a small island nation. The MFA's 53 member clubs are organised across four competitive divisions, with 10 specialist member associations handling regional and categorical competitions.

The association manages both men's and women's national teams and has invested in youth development to build a sustainable football culture on the island. In 2019, the MFA held discussions with Italian football authorities regarding the potential admission of a Maltese professional club into the Italian league system, reflecting the unique geographical and cultural ties between the two countries.

name: Moldova
content:
The Moldovan Football Federation (FMF) was established on 14 April 1990, ahead of Moldova's formal declaration of independence from the Soviet Union. The federation joined UEFA in 1993 and FIFA in 1994, enabling Moldovan clubs and national teams to compete in European and world competitions. Headquartered in Chișinău, the FMF oversees the Moldovan Liga and manages national teams across men's, women's, youth, futsal, and beach soccer categories.

The federation has invested significantly in infrastructure over the years. A UEFA-funded FMF Futsal Arena opened in 2014 at a cost of 4.2 million euros, and the FMF Beach Soccer Arena was inaugurated in August 2020. The Zimbru Stadium in Chișinău serves as the primary venue for national team matches, while the CPSM training facility in Vadul lui Vodă provides additional preparation infrastructure.

Moldovan football has faced competitive challenges at international level, with the national team yet to qualify for a major tournament. The country's football development has also been affected by the emigration of talented players to leagues in Romania and other European countries. Leonid Oleinicenco has served as federation president since 2019.

name: Montenegro
content:
The Football Association of Montenegro (FSCG) was originally established on 8 March 1931 as the Cetinje Football Subassociation within the Yugoslav football federation structure. It operated as a regional body throughout the 20th century until Montenegro's declaration of independence in 2006. The association joined UEFA on 26 January 2007 and FIFA on 31 May 2007, marking Montenegro's full integration into international football.

The FSCG is headquartered in Podgorica and manages three domestic league tiers encompassing 48 clubs, women's competitions, and national teams across multiple age groups. The federation operates Camp FSCG, described as one of the most modern training grounds in the Balkan peninsula, featuring six pitches and the House of Football administrative building, opened in 2016. Legendary former footballer Dejan Savićević has served as president since 2004.

Despite the country's modest size, Montenegrin football has produced a number of talented players who have competed at the highest levels of European club football. The national team has yet to qualify for a UEFA European Championship or FIFA World Cup, but the federation continues to develop its football infrastructure and youth pathways with growing ambition.

name: Netherlands
content:
The Royal Dutch Football Association (KNVB) was founded on 8 December 1889, making it one of the first non-British football associations in Europe. It was a founding member of FIFA in 1904 and a UEFA member since 1954. With over 1.2 million members, the KNVB is the largest sports association in the Netherlands, headquartered in Zeist.

The Netherlands revolutionised the sport in the 1970s with the philosophy of "Total Football" — a fluid, positional system in which players were capable of filling any role on the pitch. Pioneered by Ajax Amsterdam and adopted by the national team under coach Rinus Michels and captain Johan Cruyff, this approach transformed football globally. The Dutch reached the World Cup final in both 1974 and 1978, losing both times, but won their only major senior trophy at UEFA Euro 1988, defeating the Soviet Union in the final. A third World Cup final appearance came in 2010, where they lost 1–0 to Spain after extra time.

A second golden generation emerged between the late 1990s and 2014, during which the Netherlands consistently reached major tournament semi-finals and finals. The women's national team has also excelled, winning UEFA Women's Euro 2017 and reaching the 2019 FIFA Women's World Cup final. Ranked seventh globally as of April 2026, the Netherlands remains one of Europe's most consistently competitive football nations.

name: North Macedonia
description: Football Federation of North Macedonia. Founded: 1949.
content:
The Football Federation of North Macedonia was established on 14 August 1949 from a sports association of the city of Skopje within the Socialist Republic of Macedonia, initially operating as part of the Football Association of Yugoslavia. Following Macedonia's declaration of independence in 1991, the federation held its first independent assembly on 7 February 1993 and achieved full membership in both FIFA and UEFA in 1994. The organisation rebranded its visual identity in March 2014 and has been headquartered in Skopje.

A historic milestone came at UEFA Euro 2020, when North Macedonia qualified for a major tournament for the first time in their history — a landmark achievement for the nation's football. In the knockout stage they were eliminated by the Netherlands, but the campaign represented a breakthrough moment for Macedonian football, driven in part by the influence of striker Goran Pandev, the country's all-time leading scorer who announced his retirement after the tournament.

The federation oversees domestic league competition, national teams across all age groups and genders, and futsal programmes. Goalkeeper and captain Stole Dimitrievski and a new generation of players continue to build on the progress made in recent years as North Macedonia establishes itself more firmly on the European football map.

name: Northern Ireland
content:
The Irish Football Association (IFA) was established on 18 November 1880 by seven Belfast-area clubs, making it the fourth-oldest national football association in the world, after England, Scotland, and Wales. The founding members included Cliftonville, Distillery, and Moyola Park. The IFA is affiliated with FIFA and UEFA and is headquartered at Windsor Park in Belfast, which also serves as the national team's home ground.

Following the partition of Ireland in 1921, the Football Association of Ireland (FAI) was established in Dublin as a separate governing body for the south. A dispute over the venue for an Irish Cup replay between Belfast's Glentoran and Dublin's Shelbourne triggered the split, with the IFA refusing to relocate the match as promised. From 1950 onwards the IFA focused exclusively on Northern Ireland, and from 1954 the national team became officially known as Northern Ireland.

Northern Ireland's greatest achievement came at the 1982 FIFA World Cup in Spain, where they reached the quarter-finals — defeating the host nation Spain 1–0 in the group stage in one of the tournament's biggest upsets. Pat Jennings, George Best, and Norman Whiteside are among the most celebrated players produced by Northern Ireland. Whiteside became the youngest player ever to appear in a World Cup when he played at the 1982 tournament aged 17 years and 41 days, a record that still stands.

name: Norway
content:
The Norwegian Football Federation (NFF) was founded on 30 April 1902, when 14 delegates from three clubs met at the Hotel Bristol in Oslo and voted to establish the association. Norway joined FIFA in 1908 and became a UEFA member in 1954. With 1,814 clubs and over 373,000 registered players, the NFF is Norway's largest sports federation, headquartered in Oslo.

Norwegian women's football has achieved remarkable international success. The national women's team won the FIFA Women's World Cup in 1995 in Sweden and claimed Olympic gold at the 2000 Sydney Games. They also won the UEFA Women's Championship in 1987 and 1993, establishing Norway as one of the founding powers of the women's game. Players such as Hege Riise and Ann Kristin Aarønes were central to these triumphs.

The men's team had its finest era in the 1990s, qualifying for three consecutive World Cups (1994, 1998, 2002 as co-hosts) and producing players of the calibre of Ronny Johnsen, Ole Gunnar Solskjær, and Tore André Flo. More recently, Erling Haaland has emerged as one of the world's best strikers, though Norway has yet to translate individual talent into consistent tournament qualification. Lise Klaveness has served as NFF president since 2022.

name: Poland
content:
The Polish Football Association (PZPN) was established on 20 December 1919, building on an earlier autonomous Polish Football Union that had operated under the Austro-Hungarian Empire from 1911. The PZPN joined FIFA on 20 April 1923 and affiliated with UEFA on 2 March 1955. During the Nazi occupation of Poland in World War II, all Polish institutions were dissolved and the occupying forces banned Poles from organising football matches — a dark chapter the federation formally acknowledged at its centenary celebrations in 2019, including the honouring of national team player Józef Klotz, murdered during the Holocaust.

Poland's greatest footballing achievements came in the 1970s and early 1980s. The national team finished third at the 1974 FIFA World Cup in West Germany, with Grzegorz Lato winning the Golden Boot, and claimed bronze again at the 1982 World Cup in Spain. An Olympic gold medal followed at the 1972 Munich Games. More recently, Poland has produced Robert Lewandowski — widely regarded as one of the greatest strikers in the history of the game — who has been the cornerstone of the national team's qualifying campaigns and holds the record as Poland's all-time top scorer.

The PZPN has faced governance challenges, including a 2008 suspension by Poland's Olympic Committee for statutory violations. Cezary Kulesza assumed the presidency in August 2021 as the association sought to restore credibility and build on the footballing talent available to the national team.

name: Portugal
content:
The Portuguese Football Federation (FPF) was established on 31 March 1914 as the Portuguese Football Union by three regional associations from Lisbon, Portalegre, and Porto, adopting its current name in 1926. Portugal joined FIFA in 1923 and became a UEFA founding member in 1954. The FPF inaugurated the City of Football sports complex in 2016, built without state funding at a cost of 15 million euros, and serves as the national team's primary training and administrative base.

Portugal's greatest achievement came at UEFA Euro 2016 in France, where the national team — led by Cristiano Ronaldo — defeated host France 1–0 in the final thanks to Eder's extra-time goal. The country also won the inaugural UEFA Nations League in 2019 on home soil. Portugal hosted UEFA Euro 2004 and reached the semi-finals; at the 1966 FIFA World Cup, the team finished third with Eusébio claiming the Golden Boot — widely considered the tournament's best player.

Cristiano Ronaldo is Portugal's all-time leading scorer with over 130 international goals, making him the all-time top scorer in men's international football. Portugal has consistently produced world-class talent from its club academies, and the FPF continues to be one of Europe's stronger football-developing nations. The federation is set to co-host the 2030 FIFA World Cup alongside Spain and Morocco, a centenary edition of the competition.

name: Republic of Ireland
content:
The Football Association of Ireland (FAI) was founded on 2 September 1921 in Dublin, emerging from a split with the Belfast-based Irish Football Association (IFA). Southern clubs had grown increasingly frustrated with IFA governance centred in the north, and following the Leinster FA's disaffiliation, the FAI was established as a separate governing body. The association joined FIFA in 1923 as the Football Association of the Irish Free State, readopting its current name in 1936. Ireland became a UEFA member in 1954.

The Republic of Ireland's most celebrated era came under manager Jack Charlton, who transformed the national team into a competitive force. Ireland qualified for the FIFA World Cup for the first time in 1990 (reaching the quarter-finals), and again in 1994, as well as UEFA Euro 1988 and 2012. The passionate supporter culture developed during this period — encapsulated by the Green Army — became one of Irish football's defining characteristics.

The FAI has faced serious governance challenges in modern times. A major crisis erupted in 2019 involving hidden financial losses and a €100,000 personal loan from then-CEO John Delaney, threatening the association's viability and leading to significant restructuring under external oversight. The incident accelerated reforms and a new governance framework aimed at restoring trust and stability within Irish football.

name: Romania
content:
The Romanian Football Federation (FRF) traces its origins to October 1909, when the Association of Sports Clubs in Romania was founded in Bucharest — one of the earliest football governing bodies in Eastern Europe. Romania joined FIFA on 20 May 1923 and became a UEFA founding member in 1954. The national team made its debut on 8 June 1922, defeating Yugoslavia 2–1. Romania participated in the very first FIFA World Cup in 1930 in Uruguay, entered by King Carol II himself.

Romania's greatest achievement came at the 1994 FIFA World Cup in the United States, where the team reached the quarter-finals before being eliminated by Sweden on penalties. The squad was built around Gheorghe Hagi — nicknamed the "Maradona of the Carpathians" — alongside Florin Răducioiu and Dan Petrescu. The generation also reached the quarter-finals of UEFA Euro 2000 and produced multiple players who starred in top European club competitions throughout the 1990s.

Romania also claimed four Balkan Cup titles and won the UEFA U-18 Championship in 1962. The federation transitioned to professionalism on 1 August 1991 following the political changes of 1989. Răzvan Burleanu has served as FRF president since March 2014, overseeing the federation's ongoing development and modernisation.

name: Russia
content:
The Russian Football Union (RFS) traces its origins to 19 January 1912, when the All-Russian Football Union was established and immediately admitted to FIFA — overseeing 52 organisations across the Russian Empire. A Football Federation of the USSR was formed in 1934, admitted to FIFA in 1946 and to UEFA in 1954. Following the dissolution of the Soviet Union, the modern RFS was formally established on 8 February 1992 and reinstated in FIFA on 3 July 1992.

The Soviet national team, predecessor to Russia, won the inaugural UEFA European Championship in 1960 and reached the final again in 1988. At club level, Spartak Moscow, CSKA Moscow, and Dynamo Moscow were prominent forces in European competition throughout the Soviet era and beyond. Russia hosted the 2018 FIFA World Cup — the first held in Eastern Europe — and the national team reached the quarter-finals on home soil, eliminated by Croatia on penalties. Arshavin, Kerzhakov, and Igor Akinfeev are among the most celebrated players of the modern era.

Following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, FIFA and UEFA suspended all Russian national teams and clubs from international competition. The RFS considered transferring to the Asian Football Confederation, but voted in December 2023 to remain within UEFA, keeping the option open for future reconsideration. The suspension continues to define the federation's current standing in world football.

name: San Marino
content:
The San Marino Football Federation (FSGC) was established in 1931 and obtained FIFA and UEFA membership in 1988, enabling the tiny republic — with a population of around 35,000 — to participate in international football. Based in the City of San Marino, the federation organises the Campionato Sammarinese di Calcio, the Coppa Titano, and the Trofeo Federale super cup, as well as managing the national football team.

San Marino is the smallest UEFA member nation by both population and territory, and the national team has historically been among the most challenging sides in the world to collect points against in competitive football — rarely winning matches in UEFA qualifying. As of August 2024, FIFA officially recognised San Marino as the lowest-ranked team in the FIFA World Rankings. Despite this, the federation remains fully committed to developing football on the micro-state's territory.

One notable off-field initiative is the federation's involvement in establishing A.S.D. Victor San Marino, a club competing in Serie D — the fourth tier of Italian football — providing Sammarinese players with a higher level of competitive football than the domestic league can offer, similar to the arrangement Liechtenstein clubs have within the Swiss football pyramid.

name: Scotland
content:
The Scottish Football Association (SFA) was established on 13 March 1873 in Glasgow, making it the second-oldest national football association in the world, after England's FA. Eight founding clubs responded to a newspaper advertisement and met to create the association, initially with the aim of organising an annual challenge cup competition — the Scottish Cup, which began in 1874 and remains the world's second-oldest national cup competition. The SFA holds permanent IFAB membership, giving Scotland a permanent voice in the Laws of the Game alongside England, Wales, and Northern Ireland.

Scotland played in the world's first international football match on 30 November 1872, drawing 0–0 with England in Glasgow — a milestone in the history of the sport. The SFA joined FIFA in 1910 (with brief absences in 1920–24 and 1928–46) and became a UEFA member in 1954. Headquartered at Hampden Park in Glasgow, the association governs 125 member clubs and operates performance schools to develop young talent.

Scotland has produced some of football's most legendary managers and players, including Bill Shankly, Matt Busby, Jock Stein — who led Celtic to the European Cup in 1967 — and Kenny Dalglish. The national team qualified regularly for World Cups between 1974 and 1998 but failed to advance from the group stage on each occasion. A long wait ended when Scotland qualified for UEFA Euro 2020, their first major tournament appearance since 1998.

name: Serbia
content:
The Football Association of Serbia (FSS) traces its origins to 13 April 1919, when the Football Association of Yugoslavia was founded in Zagreb. Serbia joined FIFA in 1921, achieved permanent membership in 1923, and affiliated with UEFA in 1954. The association operated under various names through the 20th century — as Yugoslavia, then Serbia and Montenegro from 2003 — before becoming the independent Football Association of Serbia in January 2006, following Montenegro's separation. Headquarters are based in Belgrade.

The Serbian football tradition — under the Yugoslav banner — is rich with achievement. Yugoslavia reached the semi-finals of the 1930 FIFA World Cup, the first edition of the tournament, and produced multiple generations of outstanding players. During the Yugoslav era, clubs such as Red Star Belgrade and Partizan Belgrade were forces in European football, with Red Star winning the UEFA Champions Cup in 1991 — defeating Marseille on penalties in the final — one of the most celebrated moments in the region's football history.

As an independent nation, Serbia has qualified for several FIFA World Cups (2006, 2010, 2018, 2022) and continues to produce internationally recognised talent. Nemanja Vidić, Dejan Stanković, Aleksandar Mitrović, and Dušan Vlahović are among the notable players of the modern era. Dragan Džajić, a legendary former player, has served as FSS president since March 2023.

name: Slovakia
content:
The Slovak Football Association (SFZ) was originally established on 4 November 1938, joining FIFA in 1939. It ceased independent operations after World War II when Czech and Slovak football were unified under the Czechoslovak Football Association, with the two nations also fielding a combined national team. Following the "Velvet Divorce" and the dissolution of Czechoslovakia on 1 January 1993, the SFZ was re-established as an independent body, joining UEFA in 1993 and FIFA in 1994. Headquarters are in Bratislava.

The Slovak national team made an immediate impact in their early years of independent competition. Their most celebrated achievement came at the 2010 FIFA World Cup in South Africa, where Slovakia advanced from the group stage and defeated Italy 3–2 in the Round of 16 — one of the tournament's biggest upsets — before being eliminated by the Netherlands in the quarter-finals. Marek Hamšík, the country's all-time leading scorer, was the central figure of this generation and went on to become one of Serie A's most celebrated midfielders at Napoli.

The SFZ governs association football and futsal, overseeing the senior national team, youth squads from U-15 to U-21, and the women's programme. Jan Kovačík serves as the federation's president.

name: Slovenia
content:
The Football Association of Slovenia (NZS) was established on 24 April 1920 as the Ljubljana Football Subassociation, initially operating as a subnational body within the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes. It adopted its current name in 1948 when granted expanded authority within the Yugoslav football structure. Following Slovenia's independence in 1991, the NZS organised the Slovenian PrvaLiga and gained FIFA membership in 1992 and UEFA affiliation in 1993.

Slovenia's national team qualified for UEFA Euro 2000 — a significant achievement for a newly independent nation — and has since appeared at the 2002 and 2010 FIFA World Cups. The country produced several internationally recognised players in the 2000s, including Jan Oblak, who has established himself as one of the world's best goalkeepers at Atlético Madrid. At Euro 2024, Slovenia reached the Round of 16.

An important footnote in UEFA's history: NZS president Aleksander Čeferin served as the association's leader from 2011 to 2016 before being elected UEFA President, bringing significant international profile to Slovenian football governance. In 2023, the association implemented equal pay and conditions for the women's national team, reflecting a broader commitment to gender equity in Slovenian sport. Current president Radenko Mijatović has led the NZS since 2016.

name: Spain
content:
The Royal Spanish Football Federation (RFEF) was established on 29 September 1913, emerging from a merger of two rival organisations — the Federación Española de Clubs de Football (1909) and the Spanish Union of Football Clubs (1912) — that had created parallel tournaments and institutional conflict. Spain joined FIFA in 1914 and became a UEFA founding member in 1954. The national team's first official match was played on 28 August 1920 against Denmark, resulting in a 1–0 victory.

Spain is the most successful national team in the history of UEFA European Championships, having won the tournament four times: 1964, 2008, 2012, and 2024. Combined with their FIFA World Cup victory in 2010 — defeating the Netherlands 1–0 in the final with Andrés Iniesta's extra-time goal — Spain achieved an unprecedented three consecutive major international tournament victories between 2008 and 2012, a feat never before accomplished. The squad of that era, built around Xavi, Iniesta, and David Villa, and guided by Pep Guardiola's Barcelona philosophy, is widely regarded as one of the greatest international teams in football history.

The Spanish women's team won the FIFA Women's World Cup in 2023, with Spain becoming one of only two countries (alongside Germany) to have won both the men's and women's World Cups. Spanish club football, dominated by Real Madrid and FC Barcelona, has produced more UEFA Champions League titles than any other national league, reflecting the country's extraordinary depth of football excellence.

name: Sweden
content:
The Swedish Football Association (SvFF) was founded on 18 December 1904, with Sweden among the seven nations that founded FIFA that same year. The SvFF formally joined FIFA in 1907 and became a UEFA member in 1954. Based in Stockholm, the association operates through 24 district football associations and supports approximately one million members across 3,359 affiliated clubs, embodying its motto: "one club in every village, football for all."

Sweden's national team has been a consistent presence at major international tournaments. The men's team hosted and finished as runners-up at the 1958 FIFA World Cup — losing 5–2 to a Pelé-inspired Brazil in the final — and claimed third place in 1950 and 1994, as well as Olympic gold in 1948. Legends such as Gunnar Nordahl, Nils Liedholm, and Zlatan Ibrahimović — Sweden's all-time top scorer and one of the most prolific strikers in football history — have represented the country at the highest level.

The Swedish women's team has been among Europe's elite, winning the UEFA Women's Championship in 1984 and consistently reaching the semi-finals and finals of major tournaments. The SvFF won the UEFA U-21 Championship in 2015 with a squad including several future top-level professionals. Simon Åström was elected SvFF president in March 2025.

name: Switzerland
content:
The Swiss Football Association (ASF/SFV) was established on 7 April 1895, with founding members including Lausanne FCC, St. Gallen, and Grasshopper Zurich. Switzerland was one of the seven founding members of FIFA in 1904 and joined UEFA at its foundation in 1954. The association is headquartered in Bern and holds the distinction of being the first country in continental Europe to embrace football, with the sport introduced in the 1860s by English students and teachers; the Lausanne Football and Cricket Club, established in 1860, is regarded as the first football club in continental Europe.

Switzerland hosted the 1954 FIFA World Cup, at which the tournament's greatest upset occurred — Hungary's defeat to West Germany in the final, despite Hungary being considered near-invincible. The Swiss national team has been a regular presence at major tournaments in the modern era, consistently qualifying for World Cups and European Championships and making notable runs to the quarter-finals. The country has produced a number of world-class players in recent decades, including Granit Xhaka, Xherdan Shaqiri, and Yann Sommer.

Switzerland's unique geopolitical neutrality has also shaped its football institutions: FIFA has been headquartered in Zurich since 1932 and UEFA in Nyon since 1995, making Switzerland the administrative capital of world football. Peter Knäbel has served as SFA president since 2025.

name: Turkey
content:
The Turkish Football Federation (TFF) was established on 23 April 1923 — the same date as the proclamation of the Turkish Republic — joining FIFA immediately and affiliating with UEFA in 1962. The federation governs domestic football through an eight-tiered league system and manages the Turkish national team. Headquartered in Istanbul, the TFF organised a nationwide championship as early as 1924 and introduced the Millî Küme (national league) in 1937.

Turkey's finest hour came at the 2002 FIFA World Cup in South Korea and Japan, where the national team reached the semi-finals and ultimately finished third — the best result in Turkish football history. Hakan Şükür scored the fastest goal in World Cup history (11 seconds) in the third-place play-off against South Korea. The squad, featuring Rüştü Reçber in goal and a generation of players competing at top European clubs, captured the imagination of the football world.

Istanbul has established itself as a major venue for European finals: the city hosted the UEFA Champions League Finals in 2005 and 2023, and the UEFA Cup Final in 2009. Turkey will co-host UEFA Euro 2032 alongside Italy after five unsuccessful previous bids — a landmark achievement for Turkish football infrastructure. However, the federation has also faced significant controversies, including a widespread match-fixing investigation in 2011 and a 2025 referee betting scandal involving the majority of active referees.

name: Ukraine
content:
The Ukrainian Association of Football (UAF), formerly the Football Federation of Ukraine (FFU), was established on 6 March 1991 during the dissolution of the Soviet Union. It traces its roots to an All-Ukrainian Football Union founded in 1918 and the Football Federation of the Ukrainian SSR, which operated from 1959 to 1991. The UAF gained FIFA affiliation on 3 July 1992 and UEFA affiliation on 25 June 1992, and adopted its current name in May 2019 to align with European terminology.

Ukrainian club football quickly established itself on the European stage, with Dynamo Kyiv reaching the UEFA Champions League semi-finals in 1999 under coach Valeriy Lobanovskyi — himself one of the most influential tactical minds in football history. Shakhtar Donetsk won the UEFA Cup in 2009 and became a regular Champions League presence. The national team qualified for its first major tournament at UEFA Euro 2006 qualifiers and made its World Cup debut in Germany in 2006, reaching the quarter-finals with Andriy Shevchenko — one of the greatest strikers of his generation — leading the attack. Andriy Shevchenko was elected UAF president in January 2024.

Since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, Ukrainian football has operated under extraordinary circumstances. The Ukrainian Premier League was suspended and later resumed in modified form, with clubs playing home matches abroad or in safer parts of the country. The national team has continued to compete in UEFA qualifying while the war continues, serving as a symbol of national resilience.

name: Wales
content:
The Football Association of Wales (FAW) was established on 2 February 1876 at the Wynnstay Arms Hotel in Wrexham — initially to arrange a forthcoming match against Scotland — with a constitutional meeting held in May 1876 to formalise the name and governing rules. The FAW is the third-oldest national football association in the world, after England and Scotland. It is one of four founding associations of the International Football Association Board (IFAB), which oversees the Laws of the Game, and became a UEFA member in 1954. The Welsh Cup, introduced in 1877, is one of the oldest cup competitions in the world.

Wales had a long wait between major tournament appearances — qualifying for the 1958 FIFA World Cup (reaching the quarter-finals with John Charles) and then not qualifying again for a major tournament until UEFA Euro 2016, ending a 58-year absence. At Euro 2016, Wales reached the semi-finals, their best result in a major tournament, led by Gareth Bale, who became one of the finest players of his generation during his time at Real Madrid. The team qualified for the 2022 FIFA World Cup — their first since 1958 — further cementing a new era of Welsh football.

The FAW relocated its headquarters from Wrexham to Cardiff in 1985 and established the League of Wales (now Cymru Premier) in 1992 to strengthen the domestic game's independence from the English football pyramid. In 2022, the national team adopted the "Cymru" branding — the Welsh-language name for Wales — in international competition.
